_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against AGA Clinical Guidelines on Gallstone Disease, NICE NG188 Gallstone Disease, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Cholelithiasis (Gallstones): Types, Causes, Symptoms, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Cholelithiasis, the medical term for gallstone disease, is one of the most prevalent gastrointestinal disorders in developed nations — affecting 10-15% of adults and representing the most common cause of hospital admission for digestive diseases in Western countries. Gallstones form in the gallbladder when the balance of cholesterol, bile salts, and phospholipids in bile is disrupted, resulting in either cholesterol stones (80% of cases, linked to metabolic syndrome and obesity) or black/brown pigment stones (formed from calcium bilirubinate in hemolytic conditions or biliary infection). While most people with gallstones are asymptomatic and require no treatment, the 20-25% who develop symptoms face a significant risk of life-threatening complications — acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, cholangitis, gallstone pancreatitis, and cholangiocarcinoma — making elective cholecystectomy the definitive preventive strategy for symptomatic stone formers.

keyStats.prevalence: 10-15% of adults in Western countries have gallstones; prevalence rises to 30-40% in Native American and Mexican-American populations due to genetic factors affecting cholesterol metabolism
keyStats.demographics: Women are 2-3x more likely to develop gallstones than men at all ages; risk increases dramatically with age, particularly after 40 in women and 50 in men
keyStats.avgAge: Cholesterol gallstone disease peaks between ages 40-60 in women and 50-70 in men; pigment stones are more common in older adults and those with hemolytic conditions
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 20-25 million Americans have gallstones; 1-3% of asymptomatic stone carriers develop symptoms annually, translating to 1-2 million new symptomatic cases per year in the US alone

definition: Cholelithiasis (ICD-10: K80 for all gallstone disease subtypes) is the presence of stones in the gallbladder or bile ducts. Gallstones form when bile becomes supersaturated with cholesterol (cholesterol stones) or when excess unconjugated bilirubin precipitates with calcium (pigment stones). The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile between meals; when it contracts in response to dietary fat, stones may migrate through the cystic duct into the common bile duct, causing biliary colic or ductal obstruction. Asymptomatic gallstones (detected incidentally on imaging for another reason) are managed conservatively; symptomatic gallstones warrant cholecystectomy to prevent recurrent biliary pain and dangerous complications. The two main gallstone types: (1) Cholesterol stones (green, yellow, or white) — 80% of stones in Western populations, formed from precipitated cholesterol monohydrate crystals in supersaturated bile; (2) Pigment stones (black or brown) — formed from calcium bilirubinate, associated with hemolysis (black stones) or biliary bacterial infection (brown stones).

typesClassification.description: The most common type (80-85% of gallstones in developed countries). Form when bile contains too much cholesterol relative to bile salts and phospholipids — a condition known as supersaturated bile. Associated with obesity, metabolic syndrome, rapid weight loss, high-calorie diets, estrogen exposure, and genetic predisposition. Appear yellow-green on imaging.

Composed of calcium bilirubinate, calcium carbonate, and calcium phosphate. Form in the gallbladder in conditions of chronic hemolysis (sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassemia), cirrhosis, and ileal disease. The excess unconjugated bilirubin from red cell breakdown precipitates with calcium in the gallbladder.

Less common, composed of calcium bilirubinate mixed with cholesterol and calcium salts. Form primarily in the bile ducts (rather than the gallbladder), associated with biliary bacterial infection, parasitic infection (Clonorchis sinensis, Ascaris), and biliary stasis. Strongly associated with biliary tract infections and Oriental cholangiohepatitis.

Gallstones detected incidentally on imaging performed for another reason, in a patient with no history of biliary-type pain. The majority of gallstone carriers (60-80%) are asymptomatic at any given time. Watchful waiting is standard; prophylactic cholecystectomy is generally not recommended unless the patient is at very high risk of complications.

Gallstones causing episodic biliary colic — typically right upper quadrant or epigastric pain triggered by fatty meals, lasting 30 minutes to 3 hours. Pain may radiate to the right scapula or shoulder. Between episodes, patients are pain-free. Symptomatic stones warrant cholecystectomy to prevent progression to complicated disease.

primarySymptoms: Biliary colic: episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain triggered by fatty meals, lasting 30 minutes to 3 hours. The pain is characteristically severe, steady (not cramping), and may radiate to the right scapula or right shoulder tip — referred pain via the phrenic nerve.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying biliary colic — triggered by the visceral nociception of gallbladder distension. Vomiting often provides partial pain relief.
Post-prandial fullness and dyspepsia — a feeling of slow digestion, epigastric heaviness, and early satiety after fatty meals. Nonspecific but common.
Intolerance of fatty foods — a learned avoidance behavior as patients discover that high-fat meals reliably trigger pain.
Right upper quadrant tenderness between episodes — not typical in uncomplicated biliary colic; persistent RUQ tenderness suggests cholecystitis rather than simple colic.
Jaundice — not a feature of gallbladder stones alone. Jaundice indicates stone migration into the common bile duct (choledocholithiasis) with biliary obstruction, and warrants urgent evaluation.
Clay-colored stools and dark urine — signs of complete biliary obstruction from a common bile duct stone, requiring urgent intervention.

Fever with biliary pain — fever is NOT a feature of uncomplicated biliary colic. Fever indicates infection: acute cholecystitis, cholangitis, or gallstone pancreatitis — all requiring urgent medical attention.
earlyWarningSigns: Pain that lasts more than 3-5 hours after onset — biliary colic typically resolves within 3 hours; prolonged pain suggests cholecystitis or pancreatitis
Pain accompanied by fever, chills, or new-onset jaundice — the 'dangerous trio' suggesting common bile duct stone with infection or pancreatitis
Pain that wakes you from sleep — biliary colic typically occurs during waking hours when the gallbladder contracts after meals; nocturnal pain is atypical
Progressive increase in attack frequency — stones migrating through the cystic duct increase the likelihood of repeated episodes and eventually complications
New intolerance to previously tolerated foods — may indicate progressive cystic duct obstruction and impending cholecystitis
emergencySigns: Right upper quadrant pain with fever and chills (Murphy's sign positive) — suggests acute cholecystitis, requiring urgent surgical evaluation
Jaundice with fever and RUQ pain (Charcot triad) — acute cholangitis, a life-threatening biliary emergency requiring urgent ERCP and IV antibiotics
Acute epigastric pain radiating to the back with nausea/vomiting — gallstone pancreatitis until proven otherwise; requires urgent hospitalization
Peritoneal signs (guarding, rigidity) with RUQ pain — possible gallbladder perforation or peritonitis
Septic shock with RUQ pain — cholangitis with sepsis requires immediate resuscitation and urgent biliary drainage

causes.description: The prerequisite for cholesterol stone formation is bile that contains more cholesterol than can be solubilized by bile salts and phospholipids. This occurs when hepatic cholesterol secretion increases (from dietary cholesterol, de novo synthesis, or adipose tissue), bile salt secretion decreases, or both. The resulting lithogenic bile allows cholesterol monohydrate crystals to nucleate and grow into stones.

A functioning, regularly contracting gallbladder empties bile effectively and prevents crystal aggregation. Gallbladder stasis — from fasting, total parenteral nutrition, pregnancy, or diabetes-related autonomic neuropathy — allows cholesterol crystals to settle, aggregate, and grow into stones. The combination of supersaturated bile AND biliary stasis is the classic two-hit model of cholesterol stone formation.

Obesity increases hepatic cholesterol secretion, reduces gallbladder bile salt pool, and causes gallbladder hypomotility — all promoting stone formation. The risk increases proportionally with BMI. Metabolic syndrome (insulin resistance, hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL) independently increases gallstone risk 2-3 fold through effects on cholesterol metabolism and gallbladder contractility.

Estrogen increases hepatic cholesterol secretion and decreases bile salt synthesis; progesterone reduces gallbladder contractility. These effects explain why women are 2-3x more likely to develop gallstones, and why risk is elevated during pregnancy (10-15% develop stones during pregnancy, most resolve postpartum), with oral contraceptive use, and with hormone replacement therapy.

Very low-calorie diets, bariatric surgery (particularly rapid weight loss from gastric bypass), and prolonged fasting mobilize peripheral fat stores, increasing hepatic cholesterol secretion while the bile salt pool shrinks. Cholesterol-supersaturated bile forms within weeks of rapid weight loss. Prevention: slow weight loss (<1.5 kg/week), adequate dietary fat (to stimulate gallbladder contraction), and ursodeoxycholic acid prophylaxis.

Gallstone disease has a strong heritable component — monozygotic twin studies show 40-50% concordance. Specific gene variants affecting cholesterol metabolism (ABCG5/8, CYP7A1, HMGCR, LDLR) and bile acid synthesis (FGF19, KLB) increase susceptibility. Certain ethnic groups (Native Americans, Mexican-Americans, Chilean Indians) have extremely high prevalence (30-70%) due to genetic predisposition to cholesterol hypersecretion.

Chronic hemolysis (sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassemia, prosthetic heart valves) produces excess unconjugated bilirubin, which is conjugated and excreted in bile, precipitating as calcium bilirubinate black pigment stones in the gallbladder. Cirrhosis similarly promotes black pigment stones through altered bilirubin metabolism and gallbladder hypomotility.

riskFactors.description: Women aged 20-60 have 2-3x the gallstone risk of men. Pregnancy, oral contraceptives, and estrogen HRT each independently increase risk. Multiparous women have higher risk than nulliparous women.
BMI >30 kg/m2 increases gallstone risk 3-5 fold; BMI >35 increases risk 7-fold. Insulin resistance and hypertriglyceridemia are independent risk factors even after adjusting for BMI.

Gallstone prevalence increases steadily with age due to cumulative cholesterol exposure, progressive reduction in bile salt synthesis, and declining gallbladder contractility. Prevalence doubles each decade after age 40.

Weight loss >1.5 kg/week precipitates gallstone formation in 30-50% of patients within 3-6 months. All patients undergoing bariatric surgery and very-low-calorie diets should receive ursodeoxycholic acid prophylaxis.
First-degree relatives of gallstone carriers have a 1.5-2 fold increased risk. The heritability of gallstone susceptibility is well-established in twin studies.

These populations have genetic variants in cholesterol transporter genes (ABCG5/8) causing cholesterol hypersecretion into bile, resulting in gallstone prevalence of 30-70% — the highest in the world.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged fasting or total parenteral nutrition
riskFactors.description: NPO status or TPN eliminates enteral stimulation of gallbladder contraction, causing bile stasis. Gallstones can form within 3-6 weeks of TPN. Small amounts of enteral nutrition or prophylactic ursodeoxycholic acid is recommended in prolonged TPN patients.

Gallbladder sludge and stones develop in 10-15% of pregnant women, primarily in the third trimester from estrogen-driven cholesterol hypersecretion and progesterone-driven gallbladder hypomotility. Most cases resolve spontaneously postpartum.
Sickle cell disease, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassemia, and chronic transfusion therapy cause black pigment stones through chronic bilirubin overload of bile.

Fibrate medications (gemfibrozil, fenofibrate) increase cholesterol excretion into bile. Octreotide reduces gallbladder contractility. Ceftriaxone can precipitate in bile as calcium salts, forming sludge and stones.

affectedDemographics: Gallstone disease affects 10-15% of adults in Western countries — making it one of the most prevalent digestive diseases and the leading cause of digestive disease hospitalization
Women aged 20-60 are at highest risk, with a female-to-male ratio of 2-3:1 that narrows after menopause
Native American populations have the world's highest gallstone prevalence (30-70% of adults), followed by Mexican-Americans (15-20%)
The rising prevalence of obesity and metabolic syndrome in developing countries is increasing gallstone rates globally
Bariatric surgery patients develop gallstones at very high rates (up to 50%) without prophylactic measures due to rapid weight loss
Asymptomatic gallstones are 3x more common than symptomatic stones — most people with gallstones never develop complications
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cholelithiasis is primarily imaging-based. Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line diagnostic test — it has >95% sensitivity and specificity for gallstones >3 mm in diameter, can assess gallbladder wall thickness, detect pericholecystic fluid, and identify signs of cholecystitis. Ultrasound is non-invasive, inexpensive, requires no radiation, and requires no preparation. CT is less sensitive for gallstones (50-75%) because many stones are isodense to bile — it is used when complications (perforation, pancreatitis) are suspected and ultrasound is negative. MRCP is the test of choice when common bile duct stones are suspected based on abnormal LFTs or dilated bile ducts on ultrasound. The clinical challenge is distinguishing true biliary pain from dyspepsia, IBS, and peptic ulcer disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line diagnostic test for gallstones. Detects stones >3 mm with >95% sensitivity and specificity. Assesses gallbladder wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, stone location, and signs of acute cholecystitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute non-invasive scan. Patient fasts for 8 hours prior to optimize gallbladder visualization. A transducer is passed over the RUQ. No radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In uncomplicated cholelithiasis, LFTs are typically normal. Elevated ALP, GGT, and direct bilirubin suggest choledocholithiasis (CBD stone) with biliary obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results available within hours. Ordered routinely with abdominal ultrasound to assess for ductal involvement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive imaging of the bile and pancreatic ducts. Indicated when clinical suspicion of common bile duct stone exists (dilated ducts on ultrasound, elevated bilirubin, elevated ALP) but confirmation is needed before therapeutic ERCP.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute MRI scan. No radiation, no contrast required for biliary imaging. Highly accurate (>95%) for CBD stones.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High-frequency ultrasound from within the GI tract detects small gallstones and microlithiasis that transabdominal ultrasound misses. Used when transabdominal US is negative but clinical suspicion of biliary disease is high.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An endoscopic procedure with sedation. The echoendoscope is passed through the mouth to the duodenum, positioned at the gallbladder.

diagnosticTests.test: HIDA scan (hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid scan, cholescintigraphy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: A nuclear medicine scan that assesses gallbladder function and cystic duct patency. The gallbladder fills with a radioactive tracer excreted in bile; failure to visualize the gallbladder at 4 hours indicates cystic duct obstruction — confirming acute cholecystitis with >95% sensitivity when ultrasound is equivocal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An IV injection of a radiotracer followed by imaging over 4 hours. A positive test (non-filling gallbladder) confirms cystic duct obstruction.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Less sensitive for gallstones than ultrasound but useful for evaluating complications: gallbladder wall gas (emphysematous cholecystitis), pericholecystic fluid, perforation, pancreatitis, or an alternative diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute CT scan with IV contrast. Involves radiation exposure. Not the primary gallstone diagnostic test.

treatmentOverview: Asymptomatic gallstones discovered incidentally do not require treatment — the risk of surgical complications (bile duct injury, 0.1-0.5%; mortality 0.03-0.3%) outweighs the annual risk of developing symptoms (1-3%) in most patients. Prophylactic cholecystectomy is reserved for high-risk groups. For symptomatic cholelithiasis, laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the definitive treatment — removal of the gallbladder via four small incisions under general anesthesia. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy has a 95% success rate with low complication rates. Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 10-15 mg/kg/day is a medical alternative for patients who are poor surgical candidates or refuse surgery — it dissolves cholesterol stones slowly over 6-24 months but only works in 30-40% of selected patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 10-15 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: A hydrophilic bile acid that reduces cholesterol secretion into bile, promotes cholesterol stone dissolution, and prevents new stone formation. Indicated for patients with radiolucent (cholesterol) stones <5 mm in a functioning gallbladder who are poor surgical candidates or decline surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dissolution rates of 30-40% in carefully selected patients over 12-24 months. Stones recur in 50% of patients within 5 years of stopping UDCA. Not suitable for pigment stones or large calcified stones.

medicalTreatments.name: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
medicalTreatments.description: Removal of the gallbladder through four small (0.5-1.5 cm) abdominal incisions using a laparoscope and specialized instruments. The gold-standard definitive treatment for symptomatic cholelithiasis. Performed under general anesthesia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 95% success rate with resolution of biliary pain in >95% of patients. Low complication rate (bleeding, infection, bile leak 1-2%). Postcholecystectomy syndrome occurs in 5-10%.

medicalTreatments.name: ERCP with sphincterotomy and CBD stone extraction
medicalTreatments.description: Removal of common bile duct stones via endoscopy before or after cholecystectomy. A duodenoscope is passed to the duodenum; the sphincter of Oddi is cut; and stones are extracted with a balloon or basket.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CBD stone clearance in >90% of cases. Post-ERCP pancreatitis is the main complication (2-5%). ERCP + cholecystectomy is the standard management for confirmed choledocholithiasis.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for acute cholecystitis
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or carbapenems) are given for acute cholecystitis while preparing for urgent cholecystectomy. Surgery should ideally be performed within 72 hours of symptom onset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotics are adjunctive to surgery — they reduce the systemic inflammatory response and wound infection risk but do not treat the underlying stone obstruction.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: The standard of care for symptomatic gallstones. Four small incisions; laparoscope and instruments introduced; gallbladder removed via the umbilical port. Can be performed as an outpatient procedure (same-day discharge) in low-risk patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: >95% of cases completed laparoscopically; 1-5% require conversion to open surgery (typically due to severe inflammation, adhesions, or anatomical variation). Mortality <0.1% in elective cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Single-incision laparoscopic cholecystectomy (SILC)
surgicalOptions.description: A variant of laparoscopic cholecystectomy performed through a single incision at the umbilicus, leaving a virtually scarless result. Technically demanding; requires specialized ports and instrumentation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Similar success and complication rates to standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy in experienced hands. Cosmesis is superior. Not suitable for all patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Open cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Traditional gallbladder removal through a right subcostal or midline abdominal incision. Required when laparoscopic cholecystectomy cannot be completed safely (severe inflammation, unclear anatomy, major bleeding, bile duct injury recognized intraoperatively, cirrhosis with portal hypertension).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive when indicated. Higher morbidity than laparoscopic surgery due to the conditions requiring conversion.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placement
surgicalOptions.description: Image-guided (ultrasound/fluoroscopy) insertion of a drainage catheter into the gallbladder through the liver to decompress an infected, obstructed gallbladder in patients too ill for immediate surgery. The tube drains infected bile externally, controlling sepsis while the patient is stabilized for delayed cholecystectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective at controlling sepsis in 80-90% of critically ill patients with acute cholecystitis. Allows bridge to definitive cholecystectomy when the patient is stabilized.

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting for asymptomatic gallstones
alternativeTreatments.description: Studies consistently show that the annual risk of developing biliary symptoms is only 1-3%, and the risk of life-threatening complications is <0.5% per year in asymptomatic stone carriers. Watchful waiting is safe and standard of care for the majority who never develop problems.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate in 80% of gallstone carriers. Avoids surgical morbidity entirely. Annual follow-up is recommended to identify any change in symptoms.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary fat stimulation of gallbladder emptying
alternativeTreatments.description: Small amounts of dietary fat at each meal stimulate cholecystokinin release and regular gallbladder contraction, preventing bile stasis and stone formation. Skipping meals and very low-fat diets promote biliary stasis.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Not a treatment for existing stones but important for preventing progression from sludge to stones in high-risk patients (TPN, rapid weight loss).

alternativeTreatments.name: Prophylactic UDCA during rapid weight loss
alternativeTreatments.description: Patients undergoing bariatric surgery or very-low-calorie diets should receive UDCA 500-600 mg twice daily to prevent gallstone formation. UDCA reduces cholesterol saturation of bile and inhibits cholesterol crystal nucleation.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces gallstone formation by 50-70% during rapid weight loss. Standard of care in bariatric surgery programs. Should be continued until weight stabilizes.

whySeeSpecialist: Referral to a general surgeon (for cholecystectomy) or gastroenterologist (for diagnostic evaluation and medical management) is warranted when a patient with known gallstones develops characteristic biliary pain (RUQ pain after fatty meals), complications (jaundice, fever, pancreatitis), or requires evaluation for possible CBD stones. Urgent referral is needed for Charcot triad, gallstone pancreatitis, or acute cholecystitis.
doctorSelectionGuide: For symptomatic gallstones requiring surgery, seek a board-certified general surgeon with high volume cholecystectomy experience (surgeons performing >100 cholecystectomies per year have lower bile duct injury rates). Ask about their conversion rate from laparoscopic to open surgery (should be <5%), their bile duct injury rate (should be <0.5%), and whether they offer same-day laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight — obesity is the strongest modifiable risk factor for cholesterol gallstone formation
Avoid rapid weight loss — lose weight gradually at <1.5 kg/week and include adequate dietary fat (20-30g/day) to stimulate gallbladder contraction
Take UDCA prophylaxis if undergoing bariatric surgery or very-low-calorie diet — reduces gallstone formation by 50-70%
Eat regular meals with adequate fat — skipping meals and very low-fat diets cause bile stasis and stone formation
Consider UDCA if starting estrogen therapy or oral contraceptives in a high-risk patient
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a Mediterranean-style diet — monounsaturated fats (olive oil, nuts), fiber, and moderate red wine consumption are associated with reduced gallstone risk
Exercise regularly (150 minutes/week) — physical activity reduces gallstone risk by 20-30%, independent of weight loss
Limit high-calorie, high-fat, and processed food diets — these drive metabolic syndrome and cholesterol supersaturation of bile
Stay hydrated — adequate water intake supports bile flow and may reduce stone formation risk
Limit alcohol to moderate amounts — heavy alcohol use causes cirrhosis and secondary pigment stones

recommended: Moderate total fat intake (20-30% of calories) — dietary fat stimulates CCK release, promoting gallbladder emptying and preventing bile stasis; extremely low-fat diets paradoxically increase gallstone risk
High-fiber foods (whole grains, legumes, vegetables) — fiber binds bile acids in the intestine and may reduce cholesterol saturation of bile
Monounsaturated fats (olive oil, avocado, nuts) — may reduce biliary cholesterol compared to saturated and trans fats
Lean protein sources (fish, poultry, plant proteins) — preferable to very high-fat protein sources
Gradual weight loss if overweight — sustainable weight loss of 0.5-1 kg/week with adequate fat intake
avoid: Very low-fat diets (<10% of calories) — cause prolonged gallbladder stasis and increase stone formation risk during weight loss
Rapid weight loss diets (VLCDs, very-low-calorie diets) — without UDCA prophylaxis, these cause gallstone formation in 30-50% of dieters within 3-6 months
Highly processed foods and refined carbohydrates — drive metabolic syndrome, hyperinsulinemia, and cholesterol hypersecretion into bile
Excessive saturated and trans fats — increase biliary cholesterol saturation
Skipping meals and prolonged fasting — no enteral stimulation of CCK means no gallbladder contraction, leading to bile stasis
Excessive alcohol — heavy drinking causes cirrhosis, which is a risk factor for pigment gallstones

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise reduces gallstone risk by 20-30% in epidemiological studies, independent of weight loss — likely through improved insulin sensitivity, reduced triglycerides, and enhanced gallbladder contractility. The recommendation is 150 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise per week (brisk walking, cycling, swimming). After cholecystectomy, there are no long-term exercise restrictions — patients return to full activity within 2-4 weeks.
dailyManagement: If you have asymptomatic gallstones, maintain awareness of the character of biliary pain so you can recognize if symptoms develop — RUQ/epigastric pain after fatty meals lasting >30 minutes is the key trigger
Avoid very low-fat diets — include at least 20-30g of fat per day to stimulate regular gallbladder emptying and prevent bile stasis
If you have symptomatic gallstones and are awaiting surgery, avoid fatty meals to reduce the frequency of biliary colic episodes
After cholecystectomy, resume a normal diet — bile drips continuously into the intestine from the cystic duct remnant
Report new-onset jaundice, fever, or pain radiating to the back immediately — these indicate possible CBD stone, cholangitis, or pancreatitis requiring urgent evaluation
prognosis: The prognosis for uncomplicated cholelithiasis is excellent — most people with gallstones never develop symptoms and die of unrelated causes. Once symptoms develop, the natural history shifts: without cholecystectomy, 70% of patients have recurrent pain, 20-40% develop complications (cholecystitis, CBD stones, pancreatitis) within 2 years. Cholecystectomy is curative — it eliminates biliary colic and prevents all gallbladder-related complications. Mortality from elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy is <0.1%; mortality from emergency cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis is 5-10x higher.
recoveryTimeline: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy: most patients go home the same day or next morning. Return to desk work in 1 week; return to full activity including exercise in 2-4 weeks. UDCA dissolution therapy: stone dissolution takes 6-24 months of continuous daily treatment; recurrence after stopping occurs in 50% of patients within 5 years. Acute cholecystitis with early surgery (within 72 hours): resolution of acute inflammation over 1-2 weeks; full recovery in 4-6 weeks.
complications: Acute cholecystitis — inflammation and infection of the gallbladder wall from persistent cystic duct obstruction. Occurs in 1-3% of symptomatic gallstone carriers per year. Presents with fever, RUQ tenderness, and positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Requires urgent antibiotics and cholecystectomy.

Choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stones) — stones that migrate from the gallbladder into the CBD, causing biliary obstruction. Occurs in 10-15% of gallstone patients. Presents with jaundice, elevated bilirubin, and elevated ALP. Risk of ascending cholangitis and gallstone pancreatitis.

Gallstone pancreatitis — a stone impacted in the distal CBD or ampulla of Vater blocking pancreatic duct outflow, triggering acute pancreatitis. The most feared complication of gallstones; mortality 1-5% for mild cases, 20-30% for severe necrotizing pancreatitis.
Ascending cholangitis — infection of an obstructed bile duct. Life-threatening if not treated with urgent biliary drainage.

Gallstone ileus — a large gallstone erodes through the gallbladder wall into the adjacent intestine (duodenum), obstructing the bowel lumen. A complication of chronic cholecystitis with fistula formation. Requires emergency surgery.

Emphysematous cholecystitis — a rare, life-threatening variant where gas-forming organisms infect the gallbladder wall, causing gangrene and rapid perforation. More common in diabetics. Higher mortality than standard cholecystitis.

Porcelain gallbladder — calcification of the gallbladder wall associated with chronic cholecystitis. Historically linked to gallbladder cancer risk, though recent data suggests the risk is lower than previously thought. Prophylactic cholecystectomy is often recommended.

Postcholecystectomy syndrome — persistent abdominal symptoms after cholecystectomy, occurring in 5-10% of patients. Usually reflects pre-existing functional GI disorders rather than surgical complications.

supportResources.name: American Gastroenterological Association — Gallstone Disease Patient Resource
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on gallstone disease, treatment options, and when to consider surgery, from the AGA.

supportResources.name: NICE Guideline NG188: Gallstone Disease. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2021.
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline covering diagnosis, management, and timing of cholecystectomy for gallstone disease.

supportResources.name: Gallstones and Cholecystitis. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases (NIDDK); 2023.
supportResources.description: Government patient education resource covering gallstone types, risk factors, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Cholecystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder from persistent cystic duct obstruction, presenting with fever, RUQ tenderness, and positive Murphy's sign. Uncomplicated cholelithiasis causes episodic biliary colic without fever or persistent tenderness. Cholecystitis is a complication of cholelithiasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Choledocholithiasis (Common Bile Duct Stones)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stones in the common bile duct (CBD) cause jaundice, elevated bilirubin, and elevated ALP. Gallstones in the gallbladder alone do not cause jaundice. Choledocholithiasis is a complication of cholelithiasis requiring ERCP management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcers cause epigastric pain that may be triggered by meals (gastric ulcers) or relieved by meals (duodenal ulcers). Gallstone pain is RUQ-predominant, triggered specifically by fatty meals, and radiates to the scapula.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS causes chronic abdominal pain with altered bowel habits. Gallstone pain is episodic, meal-related, and does not affect bowel pattern. IBS and gallstones are both common and can coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Cholecystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute inflammation of the gallbladder — the most common complication of symptomatic cholelithiasis. Requires urgent antibiotics and cholecystectomy.

relatedConditions.name: Choledocholithiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Gallstones in the common bile duct — the most dangerous complication of cholelithiasis. Can cause jaundice, cholangitis, and gallstone pancreatitis. Requires ERCP for stone extraction.

relatedConditions.name: Gallstone Pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most feared complication of cholelithiasis. A gallstone obstructs the pancreatic duct outlet, triggering acute pancreatitis. All survivors need cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Cholangitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Ascending cholangitis can result from a CBD stone obstructing and infecting the bile ducts. Life-threatening emergency requiring ERCP and antibiotics.

faqs.question: Do all gallstones need to be treated?
faqs.answer: No — the majority of people with gallstones (60-80%) are asymptomatic and never develop problems. Watchful waiting is standard care for asymptomatic gallstones. Treatment (cholecystectomy) is recommended when gallstones become symptomatic, when complications develop (cholecystitis, pancreatitis, cholangitis), or in very high-risk patients.

faqs.question: What is the best way to remove gallstones?
faqs.answer: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) is the definitive treatment for symptomatic gallstones. Removing the gallbladder eliminates the risk of all gallstone-related complications. Ursodeoxycholic acid can dissolve some cholesterol stones but takes 6-24 months, only works in 30-40% of selected patients, and stones recur in 50% after stopping.

faqs.question: Can I live without a gallbladder?
faqs.answer: Yes — the gallbladder is a storage organ, not an essential one. After cholecystectomy, bile flows continuously from the liver into the intestine rather than being stored and released after meals. Most people (90-95%) have no significant long-term digestive consequences. Some (5-10%) experience mild increased stool frequency or looser stools, particularly after high-fat meals.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with gallstones?
faqs.answer: When symptomatic: avoid high-fat and fried foods, which stimulate gallbladder contraction and trigger biliary colic. During asymptomatic periods: maintain a balanced Mediterranean-style diet with moderate fat intake, adequate fiber, and regular meals. Avoid very low-fat diets and rapid weight loss, which paradoxically increase gallstone formation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cholesterol stones and pigment stones?
faqs.answer: Cholesterol stones (80% of stones in Western populations) are yellow-green, formed from precipitated cholesterol monohydrate crystals when bile is supersaturated with cholesterol. Pigment stones (black or brown) are composed of calcium bilirubinate. Black pigment stones form in the gallbladder in hemolytic conditions. Brown pigment stones form in the bile ducts from biliary infection.

faqs.question: Does weight loss cause gallstones?
faqs.answer: Yes — rapid weight loss is a major risk factor for gallstone formation. When fat is metabolized rapidly, the liver secretes excess cholesterol into bile while the bile salt pool shrinks, creating supersaturated bile. 30-50% of people on very-low-calorie diets develop gallstones within 3-6 months without prophylaxis. Slow weight loss, adequate dietary fat, and ursodeoxycholic acid during weight loss programs reduce this risk.

faqs.question: What happens if a gallstone is left untreated?
faqs.answer: In asymptomatic patients, most gallstones remain silent and cause no harm. In symptomatic patients, the natural history without treatment is: 70% have recurrent pain, 20-40% develop a complication (cholecystitis, CBD stone, pancreatitis, cholangitis) within 2 years. The most dangerous complication is gallstone pancreatitis, which has 1-5% mortality even in mild cases.

faqs.question: How are gallstones diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line diagnostic test — it detects gallstones with >95% sensitivity and can assess the gallbladder wall and bile ducts. Blood tests (LFTs, bilirubin) check for bile duct obstruction. MRCP is used when a common bile duct stone is suspected. CT is less sensitive for stones but useful for complications.

faqs.question: Can gallstones cause cancer?
faqs.answer: Gallstones themselves are not cancerous, but chronic cholelithiasis and cholecystitis are associated with an increased risk of gallbladder cancer (2-3 fold elevated risk). The risk is highest in patients with porcelain gallbladder (calcified gallbladder wall), large stones (>3 cm), and chronic inflammation. Prophylactic cholecystectomy is often recommended in these higher-risk scenarios.

faqs.question: Are there medications to dissolve gallstones?
faqs.answer: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) can dissolve some cholesterol gallstones over 6-24 months of daily treatment. It only works for radiolucent (cholesterol) stones <5 mm in a functioning gallbladder with a patent cystic duct — roughly 30-40% of gallstone patients meet these criteria. Stones recur in 50% of successfully treated patients within 5 years. Surgery is almost always preferred over medical dissolution when symptoms are present.

faqs.question: What is the connection between pregnancy and gallstones?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy dramatically increases gallstone risk through hormonal mechanisms: estrogen increases cholesterol secretion into bile; progesterone reduces gallbladder contractility. 10-15% of pregnant women develop gallstone sludge or stones, most during the third trimester. Most cases resolve spontaneously after delivery. Symptomatic gallstones during pregnancy are managed conservatively when possible; laparoscopic cholecystectomy in the second trimester is safe when needed.
